Do not include marketing emails from the library system. Filter on actual transactional language.
expectedOutput: A Sunday evening email with two sections (due soon, holds ready) or a single-line 'nothing this week' note when the inbox is quiet.
customization: Edit the search terms to match your library's specific phrasing, narrow the 21-day window, or change cron to daily.

name: Trash Night Reminder
prompt:
Send a trash night SMS reminder.

1. Compose this exact text: 'Trash night. Bins to the curb before bed.'
2. Send the SMS via send_sms_to_user with no contact_name so it routes to the account owner.
3. Do not check weather, calendar, or any other context. Do not add a second line.

The reminder works because it is boring and identical every time. If you start improvising copy, you train the brain to skim past it. Keep the text byte-for-byte stable across runs.
expectedOutput: A one-line SMS at 8pm Sunday and Wednesday with the exact text above and nothing else.
customization: Change the cron days to your local pickup schedule, or add recycling on alternate weeks.

name: Family Birthday Two-Week Warning
prompt:
Warn me about family birthdays exactly 14 days out.

1. Use use_app_google_calendar with the google-calendar-list-events tool. Source calendar: 'Birthdays' (fall back to 'Family' if the first does not exist).
2. Time window: events starting 14 days from today through 15 days from today (a 24-hour slice).
3. Count matching events:
   a. Zero matches: send nothing, exit
   b. One match: continue
   c. Two or more: still send, but list all of them in one email
4. For each match, generate:
   a. Three gift ideas under $50, specific to what you know about the person if their name appears in /home/workspace/Persona/people/ notes, otherwise generic but tasteful
   b. A draft 2-3 sentence text message they could send on the actual day
5. Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Birthday in 14 days: <name>' (use the first name if multiple). Body contains a section per person with gift ideas as a bulleted list and the draft text in a blockquote.

Do not include Amazon affiliate links or generic 'thoughtful card' filler.
expectedOutput: A morning email exactly 14 days before each family birthday with three gift ideas per person and a draft message in a blockquote.
customization: Rename the source calendar, change the 14-day lead to 7 or 30, or raise the gift budget above $50.

name: Friday Restaurant Pick
prompt:
Pick a Friday night restaurant without repeating yourself.

1. Read /home/workspace/Notes/restaurant-history.md before searching. This file holds prior picks with dates.
2. Use web_search to find one well-reviewed restaurant within 15 minutes of the stored home location.
3. Filters:
   a. Rating 4.4+ stars
   b. At least 50 reviews
   c. Not present in restaurant-history.md within the last 8 weeks
4. If no candidate meets all three filters, fall back to dropping the review-count floor to 25 before giving up.
5. Append the new pick to restaurant-history.md with today's date, name, and cuisine.
6. SMS me via send_sms_to_user with no contact_name. Format: '<Name> (<cuisine>). <wait estimate if available, else address>.' Keep it under 160 chars.

Do not pick chains. If a result is a franchise, skip it.
expectedOutput: A Friday afternoon SMS with one restaurant name, cuisine, and either a wait estimate or address, plus an appended line in restaurant-history.md.
customization: Adjust the 4.4-star floor, the 15-minute radius, or the 8-week rotation cooldown.

name: Sunday Meal Prep List
prompt:
Plan the week's dinners from the meal rotation.

1. Read /home/workspace/Home/meal-rotation.md. It contains a markdown table of 14 recurring meals with ingredient lists.
2. Read /home/workspace/Home/meal-history.md to see what has been picked in the last 3 weeks.
3. Select 4 dinners and 2 lunches that do NOT appear in meal-history.md within the last 3 weeks. If fewer than 6 eligible meals remain, drop the cooldown to 2 weeks for this run only.
4. Build a consolidated shopping list:
   a. Pull ingredients from all 6 selected meals
   b. Deduplicate by ingredient name
   c. Sum quantities where units match, list separately where they do not
5. Build a prep order section: what to roast first (longest oven time), what to chop in batch, what to portion into containers.
6. Append the 6 selected meals with today's date to meal-history.md.
7. Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Sunday meal prep, week of <date>'. Body has three sections: Picks, Shopping List, Prep Order.

Do not invent recipes. Only use meals already in meal-rotation.md.
expectedOutput: A Sunday morning email with three sections (meal picks, deduplicated shopping list, prep order) and an updated meal-history.md file.
customization: Edit meal-rotation.md to change the meal pool, or adjust the 3-week cooldown window.

name: Pollen Count Morning Alert
prompt:
Check today's pollen count and send an SMS only when it actually matters.

1. Use web_search with the query 'pollen count today Salmon Idaho' from pollen.com and weather.com. Cross-check both if the first source is stale.
2. Parse three values: tree, grass, weed. Each will be Low, Medium, High, or Very High.
3. Decision rule: if any of the three is High or Very High, continue. If all are Low or Medium, stop and send nothing.
4. Build a single SMS that names which category is high and one practical action. Pick the action by category:
   - Tree: 'Close windows before 10am, rinse hair before bed.'
   - Grass: 'Take antihistamine before going outside, change clothes when you come back in.'
   - Weed: 'Mask up for yard work, dry laundry indoors.'
5. Text me via send_sms_to_user with no contact_name. Format: 'Pollen alert: [type] is [level]. [Action].'

Do not send a 'nothing to report' message on quiet days. Silence is the feature.
expectedOutput: A single SMS on high-pollen mornings, naming which pollen is bad and one concrete countermeasure. No message on Low or Medium days.
customization: Change the threshold to also alert on Medium, or extend to year-round.

name: Oil Change Mileage Watch
prompt:
Project current vehicle mileage and email me only when an oil change is close.

1. Read /home/workspace/Home/vehicle-log.md. Expect fields: last_odometer, last_reading_date, miles_per_month, last_oil_change_odometer, interval (default 5000).
2. Compute projected_mileage = last_odometer + (months_since_last_reading * miles_per_month). Round to nearest 10.
3. Compute next_oil_change_target = last_oil_change_odometer + interval.
4. If projected_mileage is within 500 miles of next_oil_change_target (or already past it), continue. Otherwise stop.
5. Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Oil change due soon: ~[projected] mi'. Body should include:
   - Last logged reading and date
   - Projected current mileage
   - Target mileage for next change
   - Miles remaining (can be negative)
   - A one-line reminder: 'Update /home/workspace/Home/vehicle-log.md after the next fill-up.'

Do not send anything on months where the projection is comfortably mid-interval.
expectedOutput: A monthly email that only fires when the projected odometer is within 500 miles of the next oil change. Includes the math so you can sanity check it.
customization: Change interval to 7500 or 10000 for synthetic. Add a second interval for tire rotation if you want both tracked in one file.

name: Tax Document Round-Up
prompt:
Round up tax documents that have arrived in Gmail and flag what is still missing.

1. Use use_app_gmail with gmail-search-emails over the last 45 days. Search terms: '1099 OR W-2 OR "tax document" OR "tax form" OR 1098 OR "year-end statement" OR "tax statement"'.
2. For each hit, capture sender, subject, received date, and attachment filenames.
3. Group results by sender. Dedupe duplicate notifications from the same payer.
4. Build a checklist of common forms and mark each as Received or Missing based on the inbox scan:
   - W-2 (employer)
   - 1099-NEC (contract work)
   - 1099-INT (bank interest)
   - 1099-DIV (brokerage dividends)
   - 1099-B (brokerage sales)
   - 1098 (mortgage interest)
   - 1099-K (payment processors)
   - SSA-1099 (Social Security)
5. Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Tax docs status: [date]'. Body has two sections: 'Arrived' (grouped by sender with subject lines and attachment names) and 'Still expected' (the unchecked items from step 4).

Do not download attachments. This is a status report, not a filing system.
expectedOutput: Two emails a year (Jan 15, Feb 15) listing which tax forms have hit the inbox and which common forms have not. Grouped by sender, with a clean 'still expected' list at the bottom.
customization: Edit the form checklist in step 4 to match your actual situation. Self-employed with no W-2 should drop it. Add K-1s if you have partnership income.

name: Vet Annual Shot Reminder
prompt:
Watch the pet vet log and email me when shots or tests are coming due.

1. Read /home/workspace/Pets/vet-log.md. Expect one section per pet with fields: name, last_rabies, last_dhpp_or_fvrcp, last_heartworm_test, last_bordetella (optional).
2. For each pet, compute days_until_expiry for each tracked item using a 1 year interval from the last date.
3. If any item is within 60 days of expiring (or already past), continue. Otherwise stop and send nothing.
4. Build the email body grouped by pet. For each pet with something due, list:
   - Item name (e.g. Rabies, DHPP, Heartworm test)
   - Last date
   - Due date
   - Days remaining (negative means overdue)
5. Email me via send_email_to_user with subject '[Pet name]: vet visit due in [N] days'. If two pets are both due, use 'Lucky + [other]: vet visits due'.

Do not send 'all good' emails on months where nothing is due. The whole point is signal.
expectedOutput: A monthly email only when at least one pet has a shot or annual test within 60 days. Grouped by pet, with exact dates and days remaining.
customization: Add tests like dental cleaning or change the lead time from 60 days.

name: Plant Watering Wednesday
prompt:
Check the plant log on Wednesdays and text me what needs water.

1. Read /home/workspace/Home/plants.md. Expect a list where each entry has: name, interval_days, last_watered (YYYY-MM-DD).
2. For each plant, compute due_date = last_watered + interval_days. If due_date <= today, it needs water.
3. Build the SMS body:
   - If at least one plant needs water: 'Water: [name], [name], [name] ([N] plants).'
   - If nothing is due: 'Plants happy. Nothing due.'
4. Text me via send_sms_to_user with no contact_name.

Do not list plants that are overdue by 10+ days separately. If the log is out of date, that is a log problem, not an SMS problem.
expectedOutput: A Wednesday late-morning SMS naming exactly which plants need water this week and how many total. One line.
customization: Add a 'fertilize every N weeks' field per plant and a second SMS on the first Wednesday of the month for that. Or split tropicals and cacti into separate texts.

name: Phone Storage Cleanup Nudge
prompt:
Send a rotating monthly phone cleanup nudge over Telegram.

1. Read /home/workspace/Home/phone-cleanup-state.md if it exists. Expect a single field: last_month_sent (1, 2, or 3). If the file is missing or empty, treat last_month_sent as 3 so the next send is month 1.
2. Compute next_month = (last_month_sent % 3) + 1.
3. Select the message from this rotation:
   - Month 1: 'Phone cleanup: 1) Delete screenshots older than 30 days. 2) Clear WhatsApp media cache. 3) Review Photos shared albums for ones you can leave.'
   - Month 2: 'Phone cleanup: 1) Offload unused apps in Settings. 2) Clear Safari history and website data. 3) Delete podcast episodes you have already heard.'
   - Month 3: 'Phone cleanup: 1) Review the Downloads folder. 2) Clear voicemails older than a month. 3) Archive iMessage threads older than 90 days.'
4. Send the selected message to me on Telegram via send_telegram_message with no recipient (default account).
5. Write next_month back to /home/workspace/Home/phone-cleanup-state.md so the rotation persists.

Do not send all three messages at once. The rotation is the point.
expectedOutput: A monthly Telegram message with three specific cleanup actions, rotating across three sets so the same tasks do not repeat back-to-back. State persists in a small markdown file.
customization: Rewrite the three messages for Android (Files app, Google Photos, Play Store offload) or for your own cleanup habits.

name: Roth IRA Contribution Window
prompt:
Track Roth IRA contributions and email me a pace number during contribution season.

1. Read /home/workspace/Finance/retirement-log.md. Expect fields: tax_year, contributions (list of {date, amount}), annual_limit (default 7000, 8000 if 50+).
2. Compute total_contributed = sum of contributions for the current tax year.
3. Compute remaining_room = annual_limit - total_contributed.
4. Compute days_to_deadline. The deadline is April 15 of the year after the tax_year. In Nov-Dec, this is ~4-5 months out. In Jan-Apr, it is the prior-year deadline. Be explicit about which tax year the deadline applies to.
5. Compute monthly_pace = remaining_room / months_remaining_until_deadline (rounded to nearest dollar).
6. Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Roth IRA: $[remaining_room] left, $[monthly_pace]/mo to max'. Body should show:
   - Tax year being tracked
   - Total contributed so far
   - Remaining room
   - Days until the April 15 deadline
   - Monthly pace to max out
   - One line: 'Update /home/workspace/Finance/retirement-log.md after each contribution.'

Do not send during May-Oct. The schedule already handles that.
expectedOutput: A monthly email from November through April with exact remaining Roth contribution room, the deadline, and a per-month pace number to max it out. Reads off a single markdown log.
customization: Swap to Traditional IRA tracking by renaming the file. Add 401(k) tracking with a second section and a separate limit. Adjust the limit if the IRS bumps it.

name: Doctor Annual Physical Nudge
prompt:
Watch the annual physical log and nudge me when it has been almost a year.

1. Read /home/workspace/Health/physicals.md. Expect fields: last_physical_date, last_bloodwork_date, provider_name, portal_url.
2. Compute months_since_last_physical from last_physical_date to today.
3. If months_since_last_physical < 11, stop and send nothing.
4. If months_since_last_physical >= 11, build an email with:
   - Exact date of the last physical
   - Months elapsed
   - Date of last bloodwork (separate line, since labs sometimes lag the visit)
   - A paste-ready script: 'Hi, I am due for my annual physical. My last visit was [date]. Can we schedule a visit in the next 4-6 weeks? Standard fasting labs as well if appropriate.'
   - The portal URL from the log so you can click straight there
5. Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Annual physical: [N] months since last visit'.

Do not send a separate nudge for bloodwork unless the physical itself is also due. One nudge per cycle.
expectedOutput: A monthly email that only fires after 11 months without a physical. Includes a paste-ready portal message and the link.
customization: Drop the threshold to 6 months for chronic conditions. Add specialist visits (dentist, eye doc, dermatologist) as separate tracked fields in the same log.

name: Vacation Auto-Reply Setter
prompt:
Stage a vacation auto-reply Gmail draft on any morning a PTO event is on the calendar.

1. Use use_app_google_calendar with google_calendar-list-events for today's date range on the primary calendar.
2. Filter events whose title contains any of: 'PTO', 'OOO', 'Out of Office', 'Vacation', 'Travel'. Case-insensitive.
3. If no matching event, stop. Do nothing.
4. If a match is found, pull the event end date. If multiple matches, use the latest end date.
5. Use use_app_gmail with gmail-create-draft to save a draft in my Gmail account with:
   - to: 'self@reminder' (placeholder, never sent)
   - subject: 'OOO draft: paste into Gmail vacation responder ([start] to [end])'
   - body: 'Thanks for the message. I am out of office through [end date] and will reply when I return. For urgent items, please contact [emergency contact placeholder]. Otherwise expect a response after [end date + 1 day].'
6. Do not enable the vacation responder. Do not send anything. Leave the draft sitting in my Gmail Drafts folder for me to paste.

The whole point is that I copy the body into Gmail's actual vacation responder UI myself. Drafts are the safe path.
expectedOutput: A Gmail draft (never sent) staged any morning a PTO/OOO/Vacation event is on the calendar, with the right date range pre-filled. No SMS or email notification.
customization: Edit the body template. Or rewrite step 6 to call Gmail's settings endpoint and toggle the vacation responder directly if you trust it that far.

name: Driver License Expiry Watcher
prompt:
Watch ID expirations and email me when something is within four months of expiring.

1. Read /home/workspace/Home/ids.md. Expect entries for: driver_license, passport, vehicle_registration, concealed_carry (optional), professional_licenses (list).
2. For each item, compute days_until_expiry.
3. If anything is within 120 days (or already expired), continue. Otherwise stop.
4. Build the email body. For each expiring item, list:
   - Item name
   - Expiration date
   - Days remaining (negative if expired)
   - Renewal channel from this lookup table:
     - Driver license: 'Idaho DMV online at itd.idaho.gov or in-person Lemhi County DMV'
     - Passport: 'travel.state.gov, USPS appointment for in-person'
     - Vehicle registration: 'Idaho DMV online renewal'
     - Professional licenses: 'Issuing board website (look up in ids.md)'
5. Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'IDs expiring soon: [count] item(s)'.

Do not send 'all clear' emails. Silence means everything is fine.
expectedOutput: A monthly email only when at least one tracked ID is within 120 days of expiring. Each item gets its expiration date, days remaining, and a specific renewal channel.
customization: Add fishing licenses, CDL endorsements, or change the lead time.

name: Smoke Alarm Battery Twice-Yearly
prompt:
Send a twice-yearly smoke alarm battery reminder over SMS to me.

1. Build the message body exactly: 'Smoke alarm day. 1) Test every detector. 2) Replace any 9V older than 6 months. 3) Check the CO detector while you are at it. Reply DONE when finished.'
2. Send to me via send_sms_to_user with no contact_name.

That is the entire automation. Do not add weather context, do not add a 'have a great day' tail, do not split it into two messages. The dates line up with the daylight saving change-out tradition on purpose.
expectedOutput: A single SMS on March 15 and November 15 with a three-step checklist and a 'reply DONE' close. No follow-up logic.
customization: Move the dates if you do not change clocks. Swap the third line for whatever else you want checked on the same cadence (fire extinguisher pressure, water heater pan).

name: Refrigerator Leftover Audit
prompt:
Send a Thursday evening fridge audit nudge over SMS.

1. Build the message body exactly: 'Fridge audit: open the door, toss anything older than Sunday, plan a leftover dinner before grocery day.'
2. Send the SMS to me via send_sms_to_user with no contact_name.

One line. No second message later in the night. Thursday is chosen because most grocery runs happen Friday or Saturday and the fridge needs to be clear first.
expectedOutput: A single Thursday-evening SMS prompting a fridge sweep before the weekend grocery run. Same message every week.
customization: Move the day to whichever night precedes your grocery run. Or rotate two messages (audit one week, meal-plan-from-pantry the next) by reading and writing a small state file like the phone cleanup automation does.

name: Mortgage Payment Heads-Up
prompt:
Read /home/workspace/Finance/recurring-bills.md which lists the mortgage due date, amount, payee, and account last 4. Then:

1. Confirm the mortgage row exists and parse amount, due date, payee, and autopay flag.
2. Scan the same file for any other bill with an amount over $200 due in the next 7 days.
3. If autopay is marked, note the funding account last 4. If not, include a one-line instruction to schedule the payment manually before the due date.
4. Build a short email body: mortgage line first, adjacent large bills as a bullet list, autopay status last.

Email me with subject 'Mortgage due <date>: $<amount>' and the body above. Do not include bills under $200 and do not include anything outside the 7-day window.
expectedOutput: A monthly email five to seven days before the mortgage hits, with adjacent large bills as a bullet list and an autopay confirmation or manual-schedule note.
customization: Change the bills tracked, the lead time, or swap to SMS.

name: Streaming Subscription Audit
schedule: 0 10 1 1,4,7,10 * (10am on the 1st of Jan, Apr, Jul, Oct)
prompt:
Use use_app_gmail to pull streaming and media subscription receipts from the last 90 days. Steps:

1. Search Gmail for receipts from these senders: Netflix, Hulu, Disney, Max, Peacock, Paramount, Apple, Spotify, YouTube, Amazon Prime, Audible, Patreon.
2. For each hit, extract service name, charge amount, billing cadence (monthly or yearly), and last charge date. Normalize yearly charges to a monthly figure by dividing by 12.
3. Build a table sorted by monthly cost descending, with columns: Service, Monthly Cost, Last Charge, Cadence.
4. Total the monthly cost across all services.
5. If /home/workspace/Home/streaming-watch.md exists, identify the service watched least often and flag it as the obvious cut candidate. If the file does not exist, skip that line rather than guessing.

Email me with subject 'Streaming audit: $<total>/month across <N> services' and the table plus the cut suggestion in the body.
expectedOutput: A quarterly email with a sorted markdown table of every active streaming subscription, a monthly total, and one cut candidate when watch data exists.
customization: Add or remove sender names in step 1, or change the schedule to monthly if you churn services often.

name: Free Trial Cancel Day Alarm
prompt:
Read /home/workspace/Finance/free-trials.md, a markdown table with columns: Service, Trial Start Date, Trial Length Days, Cancel URL. Then:

1. For each row, compute days remaining as (trial start + trial length) minus today.
2. Filter to rows where days remaining equals exactly 2.
3. If zero rows match, exit without sending anything. Do not send a 'nothing to report' message.
4. If one or more rows match, build a single SMS body listing each service name and its cancel URL on its own line.

SMS me with that body. Keep it under 320 characters. Do not include rows with more or fewer than 2 days remaining, and do not re-alert on services that have already passed the 2-day mark.
expectedOutput: A conditional SMS exactly two days before any tracked free trial converts, naming the service and the cancel URL. No message at all on days with no matches.
customization: Change the lead time to 1 or 3 days, or switch to Email.

name: Daylight Saving Clock Sweep
schedule: 0 11 8 3 0 (11am on the 2nd Sunday of March, approximate)
prompt:
Send a clock sweep checklist for the spring forward and fall back transitions. Steps:

1. Build the device list in this order: oven, microwave, car dashboards, wall clocks, wristwatches, sprinkler timers, non-wifi thermostats, coffee maker, alarm clocks, smoke alarm test buttons.
2. Format as a markdown checklist with '- [ ]' in front of each device so it can be checked off.
3. Add a final line: 'Test smoke alarms while you are at it.'
4. Do not add motivational filler. The list is the artifact.

Email me with subject 'Clock sweep checklist' and the checklist as the body.
expectedOutput: A short email twice a year with a markdown checkbox list of every analog clock and timer that does not auto-adjust, ending with the smoke alarm reminder.
customization: Edit the device list, add the November date, or convert to SMS.

name: Lawn Mowing Weather Window
prompt:
Pick the best lawn mowing day from the upcoming 4-day window. Steps:

1. Use web_search to pull a 4-day forecast for Salmon, Idaho. Prefer NWS or weather.gov results.
2. For each of the next 4 days, capture: day name, rain probability, high temperature in F.
3. Filter to days with a high between 55 and 85 F.
4. From the filtered set, pick the day with the lowest rain probability. Tiebreak by earliest day.
5. If no day in the window meets the temperature band, send a one-line SMS saying so and the best-temp day as a fallback. Do not skip silently.

SMS me with the format: 'Best mow window: <day>, <rain>% rain, <high>F.' Keep it one line.
expectedOutput: A Thursday SMS with one line picking the best mow day in the upcoming 4-day window, or a fallback line if no day fits the temperature band.
customization: Adjust the temperature range, the day-of-week trigger, or the forecast horizon.

name: Family Group Catch-Up Nudge
prompt:
Read /home/workspace/Personal/family-touch-log.md, which lists each family member with their last contact date in YYYY-MM-DD. Steps:

1. Parse each row and compute days since last contact.
2. Filter to people with more than 14 days since last contact.
3. If the filtered list is empty, send nothing.
4. From the filtered list, pick the single person with the longest gap.
5. Draft one concrete opener: a question about something specific you know about their life, a shared memory, or a photo idea. Pull context from the same file if there are notes.
6. After sending, append today's date to that person's row in the file using edit_file_llm so the log stays current.

SMS me with the person's name, the day count, and the opener, in that order. One person per run.
expectedOutput: A Sunday SMS naming one overdue family member, the day count since last contact, and one specific opener. The touch log file is updated after sending.
customization: Edit the family list in the file, or change the 14-day threshold if you want tighter or looser cadence.

name: Anniversary Surprise Lead Time
prompt:
Read /home/workspace/Personal/anniversaries.md, which lists each anniversary (wedding, dating, first date, partner birthday) with month-day. Steps:

1. For each row, compute the next occurrence date and the days until it.
2. Filter to rows where days until equals exactly 21.
3. If no row matches, exit without sending.
4. For each match, draft a planning section with: the anniversary name and date, three planning prompts ('What did you do last year', 'What would surprise them', 'What would they actually want'), one reservation reminder tied to a likely venue type, and one gift idea bucket (experience, made, bought, written).

Email me with subject 'Anniversary in 21 days: <name>' and the planning section in the body. One email per matching anniversary.
expectedOutput: A conditional email exactly 21 days before any tracked anniversary, with the date, three planning prompts, a reservation nudge, and a gift idea bucket.
customization: Change the lead time, add more anniversaries, or add a 7-day follow-up.

name: Weekly Town Council Agenda Pull
prompt:
Pull the upcoming town council agenda for Salmon, Idaho. Steps:

1. Use web_search with the query 'Salmon Idaho city council agenda <current month> <current year>'.
2. Prefer results on the cityofsalmon.gov domain or the official county site. Skip news aggregators.
3. Use read_webpage on the top authoritative result to load the agenda.
4. Extract meeting date, time, location, and the agenda item titles.
5. Build a bulleted list of agenda items. Bold any item whose title mentions zoning, taxes, budget, police, or land use.
6. If no upcoming meeting is found, send a one-line email saying so rather than skipping silently.

Email me with subject 'Council agenda <meeting date>' and the meeting header plus the bulleted agenda list in the body.
expectedOutput: A Monday email with the upcoming council meeting date, time, location, and a bulleted agenda list with hot-topic items bolded.
customization: Change the flagged keywords in step 5, or swap the city if you move.

name: Local Election Two-Week Warning
prompt:
Read /home/workspace/Civic/elections.md, which lists upcoming election dates for Idaho and Lemhi County. Steps:

1. For each election, compute days until.
2. Filter to elections where days until equals exactly 14.
3. If nothing matches, send nothing.
4. For each match, use web_search with the query '<county> <state> sample ballot <election date>' to find the official sample ballot.
5. Pull the ballot items from the official county or secretary of state result. Skip partisan or news sources.
6. Build the email body with: election name and date, polling location lookup link for Idaho (voteidaho.gov), and a bulleted list of ballot items pulled from the sample ballot.

Email me with subject 'Vote in 14 days: <election name>' and the body above.
expectedOutput: A conditional email exactly 14 days out from any tracked election, with the polling lookup link and a bulleted ballot preview pulled from the official sample ballot.
customization: Edit the elections file, change the lead time, or add early voting deadlines.

name: Stripe MRR Daily Snapshot
prompt:
Compute and log daily Stripe MRR. Steps:

1. Use run_bash_command with curl and the STRIPE_SECRET_KEY env var to hit /v1/subscriptions?status=active&limit=100. Paginate until done.
2. For each active subscription, sum the per-period amount. Convert yearly plans to monthly by dividing by 12. Convert weekly to monthly by multiplying by 4.33.
3. Append today's row to /home/workspace/Finance/mrr-history.csv with columns: date, mrr_usd, active_subs. Create the file with a header if it does not exist.
4. From the CSV, compute delta vs yesterday and delta vs 30 days ago.
5. Draw a 7-day sparkline of mrr_usd using these characters: ▁▂▃▄▅▆▇█. Scale to the 7-day min and max.

Email me with subject 'MRR: $<value> (<+/->$<day_delta> day, <+/->$<month_delta> 30d)' and the sparkline plus the active sub count in the body.
expectedOutput: A daily email with current MRR, day-over-day delta, 30-day delta, an ASCII sparkline of the last 7 days, and a CSV row appended to mrr-history.csv.
customization: Add ARR, customer count, churn count, or split by product.

name: Vercel Build Failure Watch
prompt:
Watch Vercel deploys and alert on the first failure of any deployment id. Steps:

1. Use run_bash_command with curl and the VERCEL_TOKEN env var to call /v6/deployments?limit=10.
2. Parse the JSON. For each project (group by name), keep only the most recent deployment.
3. Filter to deployments with readyState of ERROR or CANCELED.
4. Read /home/workspace/Ops/vercel-alert-state.json, a list of deployment ids already alerted on. Create it as an empty list if missing.
5. For each failing deployment whose id is not in the state file: build a one-line SMS with project name, readyState, and deployment URL.
6. Append the alerted deployment ids to the state file before the script exits. Trim the state file to the last 200 ids to keep it small.
7. If nothing failed or everything failing has already been alerted, exit silently.

SMS me one message per new failure.
expectedOutput: A conditional SMS the moment any tracked Vercel project's latest deploy hits ERROR or CANCELED, fired exactly once per deployment id.
customization: Filter to specific project names, change the polling interval, or route to Discord.

name: Cloudflare DNS Record Drift
prompt:
Detect Cloudflare DNS drift across every readable zone. Steps:

1. Use run_bash_command with curl and the CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN env var to list zones at /client/v4/zones.
2. For each zone, list DNS records at /client/v4/zones/<zone_id>/dns_records, paginating until done.
3. Build a normalized record map keyed by zone, then by (type, name) with values for content, proxied, ttl. Write the full snapshot to /home/workspace/Ops/dns-snapshot.json.
4. If /home/workspace/Ops/dns-snapshot.prev.json exists, diff the two. Classify each change as added, removed, or modified.
5. If the diff is non-empty, build an email body grouping changes by zone, each change on its own line in the format '<type> <name>: <old> -> <new>'.
6. Move dns-snapshot.json to dns-snapshot.prev.json after the diff so the next run compares against this one.
7. If the diff is empty, send nothing.

Email me with subject 'DNS drift detected in <zone>' (or '<N> zones' if multiple) and the grouped diff in the body.
expectedOutput: A conditional email only when DNS records change across any Cloudflare zone the token can read, with a per-zone grouped diff and the snapshot file rotated for the next run.
customization: Add an allowlist of record names expected to change (dynamic IPs, ACME challenges) so they do not trigger the alert.

name: NPM Audit Vulnerability Sweep
prompt:
Sweep every repo in /home/workspace/Dev/repos.txt for npm vulnerabilities.

1. Read /home/workspace/Dev/repos.txt. One repo path per line.
2. For each repo, detect the lockfile. If pnpm-lock.yaml is present, run 'pnpm audit --json'. If yarn.lock is present, run 'yarn npm audit --json'. Otherwise run 'npm audit --json'.
3. Parse the JSON. Count vulnerabilities by severity: critical, high, moderate, low. Capture the top 5 advisories with package name, severity, and whether a fix is available.
4. Write a full report to /home/workspace/Dev/audit-reports/<YYYY-MM-DD>.md. One section per repo. Include the per-severity counts and the top 5 advisories table.
5. Email me a short summary listing only repos with one or more critical or high findings, ordered by critical count desc, then high count desc. Subject: 'NPM audit: <N> repos with critical/high'. Body: a markdown table with repo, critical, high, and the top advisory.

If a repo has zero criticals and zero highs across the fleet, send an email that just says 'Clean week.' Do not skip the email.
expectedOutput: A dated markdown audit report at /home/workspace/Dev/audit-reports/ covering every repo, plus a Monday email summarizing only the repos with critical or high findings.
customization: Add pip-audit for Python repos, raise the severity threshold, or auto-open PRs for fixes.

name: SaaS Trial-to-Paid Drop-Off Watch
prompt:
Pull yesterday's expiring trial cohort from the app database and tell me who converted.

1. Connect to the app database. Use psql if DATABASE_URL starts with postgres://, otherwise sqlite3 on the file path in DATABASE_URL.
2. Find every user whose trial ended in the last 24 hours. For each, fetch: email, signup date, last login, count of core actions during the trial (the SQL fragment for core actions lives at /home/workspace/Dev/trial-events.sql, read it and inject it), and whether the user is now on a paid plan.
3. Split the cohort into two groups: Converted and Did Not Convert. Within each group, sort by core-action count desc.
4. Build two markdown tables, Converted first. Columns: email, signup date, days in trial, core actions, last login.
5. Email me with subject 'Trial cohort: <date>, <X> converted of <Y>'. Body: the two tables, plus one line at the top with the conversion rate as a percentage and the median core-action count for each group.

Do not include users whose trial ended more than 24 hours ago. Do not email when the cohort is empty, just skip that day.
expectedOutput: A daily email listing yesterday's trial-end cohort split into Converted and Did Not Convert, with per-user activity counts and the cohort conversion rate.
customization: Change the conversion windows, the event definitions, or add per-source attribution.

name: ProductHunt Top 5 Recap
prompt:
Pull the top 5 ProductHunt launches of the day and send them to Telegram.

1. Use read_webpage on https://www.producthunt.com/ to fetch today's homepage as markdown.
2. Parse out the top 5 products by upvote count. For each, capture name, tagline, upvote count, and the primary category tag.
3. Format one block per product:
   '<rank>. <name> (<votes> upvotes)\n   <tagline>\n   Category: <tag>'
4. Telegram me with all 5 blocks separated by blank lines. Add the ProductHunt URL on the final line.

If read_webpage returns fewer than 5 products, send what you got and prefix the message with 'Partial: only <N> parsed.' Do not invent entries to fill the list. Do not send anything if the page failed to load, just log the error and exit.
expectedOutput: An evening Telegram message with the day's top 5 ProductHunt launches, each as its own block with rank, name, votes, tagline, and category.
customization: Switch the delivery to Discord, change the count to top 10, or filter by a category tag like 'AI' or 'Developer Tools'.

name: Backlog Aging Heat Map
prompt:
Map how old my Linear backlog is and surface the oldest items.

1. Use use_app_linear to list every open issue in the primary team. Pull title, createdAt, assignee, priority, and project.
2. For each issue, compute days since createdAt.
3. Bucket every issue into one of: 0-7 days, 8-21 days, 22-60 days, 61-180 days, 180+ days. Count per bucket.
4. Build an HTML email body. For each bucket, render one horizontal bar made of block characters, width proportional to issue count, with the count printed at the end.
5. Below the bars, list the 5 oldest open issues. Columns: title, days old, assignee, project.
6. Email me with subject 'Backlog aging: <N> issues, oldest <days>d'. Send the HTML body inline.

Do not filter by priority by default. Old low-priority issues are exactly the thing this is meant to catch.
expectedOutput: A Monday HTML email with a bar-chart view of Linear backlog age buckets and a table of the 5 oldest open issues.
customization: Filter by project, label, or priority, and change the age buckets.

name: Cold Outreach Reply Detector
prompt:
Catch positive replies to my cold outreach within two hours and text me.

1. Read the label name from /home/workspace/Sales/outreach-label.txt. Default to 'Outreach/Sent' if the file is missing.
2. Use use_app_gmail to search for messages in that label received in the last 2 hours that are replies (the thread already had a prior outbound message from me).
3. For each new reply, classify the response into exactly one of: Positive (interested, asks a question, requests a meeting), Neutral (acknowledgment, no commitment), Negative (not interested, unsubscribe, removed list), Auto-reply (out of office, bounce, vacation responder).
4. For Positive replies only, extract the sender name, the company from the signature if present, and the first 80 characters of the reply body with newlines collapsed to spaces.
5. SMS me one message per Positive reply: '<sender> at <company>: <snippet>'. If company is unknown, drop that segment.

Do not text on Neutral, Negative, or Auto-reply. Do not text a summary count when there are zero Positives, stay quiet.
expectedOutput: An SMS within two hours of any new positive reply on a tracked outreach thread, with sender, company if known, and the first 80 characters of the reply.
customization: Change the label path, swap the cadence to hourly, or also text Negative replies if you want to follow up before leads churn.

name: Postgres Slow Query Hunter
prompt:
Find the most expensive Postgres queries from the past week.

1. Connect to the database using DATABASE_URL. Confirm pg_stat_statements is loaded. If it is not, email me 'pg_stat_statements not enabled' and stop.
2. Query pg_stat_statements for the top 10 entries by total_exec_time over the last 7 days where calls > 50. Capture: queryid, normalized query text (truncated to 300 chars), calls, total_exec_time (seconds), mean_exec_time (ms), rows.
3. Sort by total_exec_time desc. Build a markdown table with the six columns above.
4. Below the table, list any query where mean_exec_time > 500ms with a short note: 'Slow per call, not just frequent'.
5. Email me with subject 'Slow queries: top 10, <total>s total'. Body: the table, then the slow-per-call callouts.
6. Only reset pg_stat_statements if /home/workspace/Ops/reset-stats.flag exists. After resetting, delete the flag file.

Do not include queries from pg_stat_statements itself in the top 10.
expectedOutput: A Monday email with a sorted table of the 10 most expensive Postgres queries from the past week and callouts for queries that are slow on a per-call basis.
customization: Change the window, add per-database breakdowns, or route to Discord.

name: Discord Community Pulse
prompt:
Summarize last week of Discord activity per channel.

1. Read /home/workspace/Community/discord-channels.json. Expect an array of objects with channel_id and a human-readable name.
2. For each channel, use get_discord_history with limit 1000 to pull the last 7 days of messages. If the channel has more than 1000 messages in the window, note that the count is a floor.
3. For each channel, compute: total message count, unique active members, top 5 emojis used (count reactions and inline emoji), top 5 most-replied-to messages (author, first 100 chars, reply count).
4. Build an email body with one section per channel. Header: channel name and id. Then the three lists.
5. At the bottom, add a 'New Members' section listing anyone whose first ever message in any tracked channel landed in the last 7 days. Show username and the channel they first posted in.
6. Email me with subject 'Discord pulse: <total> messages across <N> channels'.

Do not include bot messages in the counts. Filter on author.bot = false.
expectedOutput: A Monday email with per-channel activity numbers, top emojis, top replied-to messages, and a callout list of new members who posted for the first time last week.
customization: Change the channels, the window, or add sentiment analysis of top threads.

name: Founder Inbox VIP Tier Sort
prompt:
Sort unread inbox by hand-built VIP tiers, twice a day.

1. Read /home/workspace/Inbox/vip-tiers.json. Expect three keys: tier1, tier2, tier3, each an array of email addresses or domains.
2. Use use_app_gmail to list unread messages received in the last 24 hours. Pull sender, subject, snippet, and received timestamp.
3. For each message, match the sender to the highest tier it qualifies for. Exact email match beats domain match. Anything not in tier1, tier2, or tier3 gets bucketed as Tier 3.
4. Build a markdown email with three sections in order: Tier 1, Tier 2, Tier 3. Each row shows sender, subject, and a single-line auto-generated reason it likely matters (pulled from the snippet, not invented).
5. If Tier 1 or Tier 2 has any entries, drop the Tier 3 section entirely. Only show Tier 3 when Tier 1 and Tier 2 are both empty.
6. Email me with subject 'Inbox: <T1> Tier 1, <T2> Tier 2, <T3> Tier 3'.

Do not mark anything read. Do not auto-reply.
expectedOutput: An email at 8am and 1pm on weekdays grouping unread mail into Tier 1, Tier 2, and Tier 3 buckets, with Tier 3 hidden whenever higher tiers have entries.
customization: Edit vip-tiers.json to change membership, add a Tier 0 for family and partner, or shift the cadence to hourly during launch weeks.

name: Weekly Competitor Pricing Page Diff
prompt:
Diff every competitor pricing page against last week's snapshot.

1. Read /home/workspace/Competitors/pricing-urls.txt. One URL per line, comments with '#' allowed.
2. For each URL, use read_webpage to fetch the page as markdown. Save the new content to /home/workspace/Competitors/snapshots/<domain>-<YYYY-MM-DD>.md.
3. Find the most recent prior snapshot for that same domain. If none exists, mark the entry as 'New baseline, no diff yet' and continue.
4. Diff the two snapshots line by line. Filter to lines that touch pricing: any line containing '$', '/mo', '/month', '/year', '/yr', 'free', 'plan', 'tier', or 'per seat'.
5. Build an email body grouped by domain. Under each domain heading, show the changed lines as 'before -> after' pairs.
6. If zero pricing-relevant changes anywhere across the fleet, email me a one-line body: 'No pricing changes detected this week.' Still send the email, do not stay silent.
7. Subject: 'Competitor pricing diff: <N> domains, <M> changes'.
expectedOutput: A Tuesday email with a per-domain list of changed pricing-relevant lines since last week, plus dated full-page snapshots saved under /home/workspace/Competitors/snapshots/.
customization: Add or remove competitor URLs, change the diff keywords, or move to daily.

name: GitHub Star Velocity Tracker
prompt:
Track GitHub star velocity for every watched repo.

1. Read /home/workspace/Dev/watched-repos.txt. Format: owner/repo, one per line. Ignore blank lines and lines starting with '#'.
2. For each repo, run 'gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>' and capture stargazers_count, forks_count, and open_issues_count.
3. Append a row to /home/workspace/Dev/star-history.csv with columns: repo, date (UTC), stars, forks, open_issues. Create the file with a header row if it does not exist.
4. From the CSV, compute 1-day, 7-day, and 30-day star deltas per repo. Compute the 30-day mean and standard deviation of daily deltas for each repo.
5. Flag any repo where today's 1-day delta is more than 3 standard deviations above its 30-day mean as 'spiking'.
6. Build a markdown table sorted by 7-day delta desc. Columns: repo, stars, 1d, 7d, 30d, spike?
7. Email me with subject 'Star velocity: <N> repos, <K> spiking'. Spiking repos at the top of the email body, then the full sorted table.

Do not flag a repo as spiking until it has at least 14 days of history. Below that the standard deviation is noise.
expectedOutput: A daily email with a sorted table of star velocity per watched repo over 1, 7, and 30 days, with spike callouts above the table.
customization: Add forks_count and open_issues_count to the velocity ranking, tighten or loosen the spike threshold from 3 sigma, or scope the list to your own repos for a personal traction view.

name: Reddit Brand Mention Sweep
prompt:
Catch new Reddit mentions of any tracked brand term within four hours.

1. Read /home/workspace/Brand/mention-terms.txt. One term per line. Expect product name, founder handle, primary domain, and common misspellings.
2. For each term, call web_search with query 'site:reddit.com "<term>"' and time_range='day'. Filter results down to ones posted in the last 4 hours based on the result timestamp.
3. Read /home/workspace/Brand/seen-urls.txt into a set. Drop any result URL already in the set.
4. For each remaining hit, Telegram me one message:
   'r/<subreddit>: <post title>\n<url>\n<first 200 chars of body or top comment>'
5. After sending, append each new URL to /home/workspace/Brand/seen-urls.txt so the next run does not duplicate.

If zero new mentions, do not send anything. The point is that silence means clean.
expectedOutput: A Telegram message within four hours of any new Reddit mention of a tracked brand term, one message per hit, with subreddit, title, URL, and a short snippet.
customization: Add Hacker News and X searches to the same loop, restrict to specific subreddits, or push the cadence to hourly during a launch week.

name: Self-Improving Prompt Library Audit
prompt:
Audit the prompt library for stale, sloppy, or undocumented prompts.

1. Use list_files to walk /home/workspace/Prompts/ recursively. Collect every .md file.
2. For each prompt file, read it and check three things:
   a. Frontmatter: does it have a 'last_used' date field.
   b. Recency: cross-reference /home/workspace/Prompts/usage-log.csv (columns: path, used_at). If the most recent entry is older than 60 days, mark stale.
   c. Slop: read /home/workspace/Prompts/forbidden-tokens.txt (one token per line, user-maintained blocklist). Case-insensitive substring match against the prompt body. Record every token hit.
3. Bucket each prompt into one or more of: Missing frontmatter, Stale 60+ days, Hits blocklist.
4. Write a full audit to /home/workspace/Prompts/audit-<YYYY-MM>.md. One section per bucket. List file paths and, for blocklist hits, the offending tokens.
5. Email me a short summary with counts per bucket and the file paths in each. Subject: 'Prompt audit <YYYY-MM>: <stale> stale, <slop> sloppy, <nofm> missing frontmatter'.

Do not auto-delete or auto-edit any prompt. This is a flagging job. Decisions stay with me.
expectedOutput: A monthly audit file at /home/workspace/Prompts/audit-<YYYY-MM>.md and a summary email naming every prompt that is stale, hits the blocklist, or is missing frontmatter.
customization: Edit forbidden-tokens.txt to tune the slop filter, change the staleness window from 60 days, or add a prompt-length cap that flags anything over a chosen character count.

name: Launch Week Engagement Decay Chart
prompt:
Read /home/workspace/Marketing/active-launches.json, which lists launch posts with post_id, post_url, launch_date, and end_date. Only proceed if today falls inside at least one active window. Otherwise stop without sending anything.

1. For each active launch post, use x_search to fetch current impressions, likes, reposts, and replies.
2. Append today's snapshot as a new row to /home/workspace/Marketing/launch-metrics.csv with columns date, post_id, impressions, likes, reposts, replies.
3. For each post, compute the daily delta in impressions vs the prior day, and find peak day so far.
4. Flag the first day where the daily delta drops below 25% of peak as 'decay onset' and note which day that was.
5. Build a per-post section with the day-by-day numbers as a short table and a 10-wide ASCII bar chart of impressions per day.

Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Launch decay: <N> active posts' and one section per launch. If there are zero active launches, do not send.
expectedOutput: A daily email, only during active launch windows, with per-post metric tables, an ASCII impressions chart, and a decay onset flag.
customization: Adjust the 25% decay threshold, add LinkedIn or Threads cross-posts, or switch delivery to Telegram.

name: Hacker News Show Pull
prompt:
1. Use read_webpage on https://news.ycombinator.com/show to fetch today's Show HN front page HTML.
2. Parse the listing and keep only items posted in the last 24 hours.
3. Sort by points descending and take the top 10. Drop anything with fewer than 10 points.
4. For each surviving item, capture title, points, comment count, submitter, the HN comments URL, and the external submission URL (the link out, not the comments page).
5. For each external URL, try read_webpage and pull a single one-line snippet of what the project is. If the fetch fails or the site blocks scraping, write 'site unreachable' as the snippet.
6. Build an email body where each item is one block: title linked to the comments page, points and comment count on the second line, snippet on the third.

Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Show HN today: top 10'. If fewer than 3 items clear the 10-point floor, send anyway and note the thin day at the top.
expectedOutput: An evening email listing up to 10 Show HN items from today, each with points, comment count, comments link, and a one-line description of the linked project.
customization: Change to Ask HN or the main front page, raise the points floor, or move to Telegram.

name: The 5pm Dinner Decider
prompt:
1. Read /home/workspace/Home/pantry.md for current ingredients and /home/workspace/Home/dietary-notes.md for restrictions and preferences.
2. Pick three dinner options that use mostly what is already in the pantry and respect every dietary note. Prefer dishes where at most two ingredients are missing.
3. For each option, list the missing ingredients (max two per dish) and a rough cook time in minutes.
4. If the pantry is effectively empty, skip step 2 and run a single web_search for one 20 minute dinner with five or fewer ingredients, and return only that.

Text me via send_sms_to_user with the three options as a tight numbered list. No preamble, no greeting, no closing line. Format each line as '1. Dish - <cook time> - missing: <items or none>'. Keep the full SMS under 320 characters.
expectedOutput: A short text at 5pm with three numbered dinner picks based on pantry contents, or a single fallback recipe if the pantry is bare.
customization: Edit pantry.md and dietary-notes.md, or change the option count and cook time ceiling in the prompt.

name: Saturday Morning Cleanup Captain
prompt:
1. Read /home/workspace/Home/cleanup-rotation.md, which groups weekly chores by room and tracks a last_done date for each room.
2. Sort rooms by last_done ascending. Pick the three rooms that have gone longest without attention.
3. For each chosen room, pull its chore list and assign an estimated minute count per chore. Aim for a total under 90 minutes across all three rooms.
4. Build a markdown checklist with one section per room. Each chore is a '- [ ]' line ending in '(<n> min)'.
5. Use create_or_rewrite_file to update cleanup-rotation.md, setting today's date as last_done for the three picked rooms. Leave the rest of the file untouched.

Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Saturday cleanup, 3 rooms' and the checklist as the body. Put the total estimated minutes on the first line.
expectedOutput: A Saturday morning email with three rooms and a timed markdown checklist, plus an updated last_done date in cleanup-rotation.md.
customization: Edit cleanup-rotation.md to add or remove rooms and chores, or change how many rooms get picked each week.

name: Birthday Card Mail-By Reminder
prompt:
1. Read /home/workspace/Contacts/birthdays.md, which lists name, birthday (MM-DD), shipping zone, and a notes column with gift ideas.
2. For each entry, compute days until the next birthday based on today's date.
3. Keep only people whose shipping zone matches the 10 day mail-by lead time. The default rule is: send a card today if exactly 10 days remain. Adjust per zone if a row specifies its own lead value.
4. If the filtered list is empty, send nothing and stop.
5. Otherwise build a markdown list. One line per person: name, birthday date, days remaining, and a one line gift idea pulled from the notes column.

Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Birthday cards to mail this week' and the list as the body. Do not send on days with zero matches.
expectedOutput: A conditional morning email, only on days when at least one person needs a card mailed today to arrive on time.
customization: Change the 10 day lead window, add per-zone lead overrides, or expand the notes column with more gift ideas.

name: Where Did I Put That Log
prompt:
This is a silent housekeeping job. Do not send any message on any channel. Save the result to /home/workspace/Home/where-is-it.md.

1. Open /home/workspace/Home/where-is-it.md. Lines look like 'passport: top drawer of camper desk' and the file is appended to throughout the day from voice notes or quick edits.
2. Parse every line as 'item: location'. If a line does not match that shape, keep it in a separate 'unparsed' section at the bottom of the rewritten file.
3. Deduplicate by item name. Keep only the most recent location entry per item, based on file order (later wins).
4. Sort the cleaned entries alphabetically by item name, case-insensitive.
5. Use create_or_rewrite_file to write back to /home/workspace/Home/where-is-it.md with a top header 'Last cleaned: YYYY-MM-DD', then the sorted list, then the unparsed section if any.
expectedOutput: A quietly rewritten where-is-it.md with deduped, sorted entries, a cleaned date header, and unparsed lines preserved at the bottom.
customization: Add category sections like 'tools' or 'documents', or shift the cleanup time to a different hour.

name: The Did I Text Mom Check
prompt:
1. Read /home/workspace/Contacts/keep-in-touch.md. Each row has name, preferred channel (text, call, email, in-person), cadence tier (weekly, monthly, quarterly), and last_contacted date.
2. Filter to rows tagged 'weekly cadence' where last_contacted is 7 or more days ago.
3. If the filtered list is empty, text me via send_sms_to_user with the single line: 'All weekly people are current.' Stop there.
4. Otherwise build one line per overdue person: '<name> - <channel> - <N>d'. Sort by days overdue, longest first.
5. Concatenate the lines into one SMS. Hard cap the total message at 320 characters. If the list overflows, keep the top entries and append '+N more' on the last line.

Text me via send_sms_to_user. No preamble, no greeting.
expectedOutput: A Sunday evening text listing weekly-cadence contacts overdue by 7+ days, or a one-line all-clear if none are overdue.
customization: Add or remove cadence tiers like 'monthly' or 'quarterly' and adjust the day threshold in the prompt.

name: Weekend Hobby Spark
prompt:
1. Read /home/workspace/Home/hobbies.md, which lists hobbies the user actually enjoys with skill level and a last_done date.
2. Compute a weight per hobby: hobbies not touched in the last 30 days get a 3x weight. Pick one hobby using weighted random selection.
3. Run a web_search for one beginner-friendly project or activity for that hobby that fits in 2 to 3 hours of actual work, matched to the recorded skill level.
4. Pull the most promising single result and extract: project name, rough time estimate, materials list, and one paragraph of instructions or a link to the source.
5. Update last_done for that hobby in hobbies.md to today's date so the rotation stays honest.

Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Saturday hobby spark: <hobby name>'. Body order: hobby, project, time, materials, instructions or source link.
expectedOutput: A Saturday morning email with one weighted-random hobby project sized to a 2 to 3 hour weekend block, plus an updated last_done in hobbies.md.
customization: Edit hobbies.md with skill levels and last_done dates, or change the time-budget bounds for suggestions.

name: Mood and Weather Pattern Log
prompt:
This automation runs in two turns. Do not skip the wait.

1. Text me via send_sms_to_user with the exact prompt: 'Mood today 1 to 5? Reply with a number and one word.'
2. Wait for an inbound SMS reply. Parse the first integer 1 to 5 as mood and the next token as the one-word descriptor. If the reply is malformed, send one clarification SMS and wait again. Give up after one retry.
3. Run web_search for today's local weather summary for Salmon, Idaho. Pull high temp F, low temp F, conditions (one phrase), and barometric pressure in inHg.
4. If /home/workspace/Health/mood-weather.csv does not exist, create it with the header row: date,mood,word,high,low,conditions,pressure.
5. Append a new row with today's date in YYYY-MM-DD format and the parsed values. Use create_or_rewrite_file to save the updated CSV.
expectedOutput: A nightly two-turn SMS check-in that appends one row per day to mood-weather.csv with mood, one-word descriptor, and four weather fields.
customization: Change the mood scale, swap the free-text question, or switch the location string used for the weather lookup.

name: Friday Fridge Cleanout
prompt:
1. Read /home/workspace/Home/fridge.md. Each line is an item with a purchase or open date, optionally tagged 'leftover', 'sauce', or 'produce'.
2. Compute age in days for each item from today.
3. Flag items at risk using these rules: fresh produce older than 5 days, opened sauces older than 7 days, anything tagged 'leftover' older than 3 days.
4. If nothing is at risk, text me via send_sms_to_user with the single line 'Fridge is fine.' and stop.
5. Otherwise pick the top five at-risk items sorted by age, oldest first. Suggest one combo dinner that genuinely uses at least two of them.
6. Build the SMS in this shape: line 1 is a header 'Use first: <N> items', then five lines of '<item> - <age>d', then a final line 'Combo: <dish using 2+ items>'.

Text me via send_sms_to_user. Hard cap at 320 characters.
expectedOutput: A Friday afternoon text with up to five oldest at-risk fridge items and a combo dinner idea, or a one-line all-clear.
customization: Edit the age thresholds per category and add new tags like 'meal-prep' or 'dairy' in fridge.md.

name: The Anti-Affirmation Scrambler
prompt:
1. Read /home/workspace/Personal/voice-lines.md, which contains 30 to 50 short lines written by the user in their own voice. Dry, honest, sometimes funny, never motivational.
2. Read /home/workspace/Personal/voice-lines-used.csv. Each row is date,line. Build a set of lines used in the last 14 days.
3. Pick one line at random from voice-lines.md that is not in the recent-used set. If every line has been used in the last 14 days, pick the line with the oldest last-used date instead.
4. Append a new row to voice-lines-used.csv with today's date and the chosen line. Use create_or_rewrite_file to write the updated CSV back.
5. Text me via send_sms_to_user with just the line itself. No quotes, no attribution, no preamble, no greeting, no closing punctuation added beyond what the line already has.
expectedOutput: A 7am SMS with one of your own one-liners, rotated so nothing repeats inside the no-repeat window, plus an updated usage log.
customization: Edit voice-lines.md to add or remove lines, or change the 14-day no-repeat window.

name: Doctor Visit Prep Sheet
prompt:
1. Use use_app_google_calendar to list events on tomorrow's primary calendar in the America/Denver timezone.
2. Scan event titles, case-insensitive, for any of: doctor, dentist, clinic, neurologist, appointment, lab, pharmacy. If no event matches, stop and send nothing.
3. For each matching event, build a one page prep sheet. Pull recent entries from /home/workspace/Health/symptom-log.md covering the last 14 days, the full current med list from /home/workspace/Health/meds.md, and the open questions from /home/workspace/Health/questions.md.
4. Format the sheet as markdown with these sections in order: Appointment (title, time, location), Recent symptoms (bulleted, newest first), Current meds (name, dose, frequency), Questions to ask.
5. Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Prep for tomorrow: <event title>' and the markdown sheet as the body. One email per matching event.
expectedOutput: An evening email the night before any matching medical appointment, with symptoms from the last 14 days, current meds, and open questions on one page.
customization: Edit the trigger keywords or the files that feed the prep sheet, and adjust the symptom lookback window.

name: The Lazy Sunday Movie Picker
prompt:
Pick three low-effort Sunday movies from my watchlist and text them to me.

1. Read /home/workspace/Home/watchlist.md. Each row has title, runtime, and tags.
2. Filter to anything under 110 minutes tagged 'comfort' or 'low effort'.
3. If fewer than three qualify, run a single web_search for one current well-reviewed feel-good movie under two hours and add it to the pool.
4. Pick three. Do not repeat anything I watched in the last 14 days (check the 'last_watched' column if present).
5. SMS me via send_sms_to_user with three lines, one pick per line, in the form 'Title (runtime)'. No preamble, no closing line.

Do not recommend anything tagged 'heavy' or 'rewatch later'. If the watchlist file is missing, text me 'watchlist.md missing' and stop.
expectedOutput: A Sunday evening SMS with three short, low-effort picks already aligned with my taste, one per line.
customization: Edit watchlist.md, change the runtime ceiling, or swap which tags count as comfort viewing.

name: Library Book Due Date Watcher
prompt:
Warn me about library books only when I am about to be late, and email me the result via send_email_to_user.

1. Read /home/workspace/Home/library-books.md. Each entry has title, branch, due_date, and an optional 'returned' flag.
2. Compute days until due for every unreturned book.
3. If at least one book is due in 2 days or less, email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Library books due soon'. Body is a bullet list: 'Title - Branch - due YYYY-MM-DD (N days)'.
4. If a due_date is missing or unparseable, list that book under a 'Check these manually' section in the same email rather than skipping silently.
5. If nothing qualifies, send nothing. Do not send an 'all clear' email.
expectedOutput: A short conditional email listing only the books within two days of going overdue, with branch and exact due date.
customization: Adjust the warning window, add a renewal link column, or split alerts by branch.

name: Camper Battery Voltage Logger
prompt:
Twice a day, ask for the camper battery voltage and log it.

1. SMS me via send_sms_to_user with exactly 'Camper bank voltage? Reply with a number like 12.6'.
2. Wait for my reply. Parse the first number in the reply as a float. If parsing fails, do nothing and stop.
3. Append a row to /home/workspace/Camper/battery-log.csv with columns timestamp, voltage, hour_slot. The 7am run writes hour_slot=morning, the 7pm run writes hour_slot=evening. If the file does not exist, create it with that header.
4. If the current month is Nov, Dec, Jan, Feb, or Mar and voltage is below 12.2, send a follow-up SMS 'Low bank, consider charging today.' For other months use 12.0 as the threshold.

Do not interpret, summarize, or graph. Just log and warn.
expectedOutput: Two daily voltage check-ins that build a CSV log and a short follow-up text when the bank is sagging.
customization: Change the thresholds per season or add columns for solar input and amp draw.

name: The Pantry Inventory Refresh
prompt:
Run a Sunday pantry pass and email me what to use and what to restock.

1. Read /home/workspace/Home/pantry.md (current items with purchase dates), /home/workspace/Home/pantry-purchases.csv, and /home/workspace/Home/shelf-life.md (expected shelf life ranges).
2. For every pantry item, compare purchase date plus expected shelf life against today. Flag anything past expected shelf life as 'Use or toss'.
3. Check the staples list (rice, beans, flour, oats, oil, salt, pasta sauce). Anything missing from pantry.md goes under 'Restock'.
4. Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Pantry pass - [today]'. Two sections only: 'Use or toss' and 'Restock', each as a bullet list. No preamble.
5. Update the 'last_reviewed' line in pantry.md to today.

If both sections are empty, still send the email with the line 'Pantry is current.' so I know it ran.
expectedOutput: A short Sunday email pairing items to use up with staples to buy, plus an updated last_reviewed date in pantry.md.
customization: Edit shelf-life.md, add or remove items from the staples list, or change which day the review runs.

name: Family Group Text Opener
prompt:
Draft one paste-ready opener for the family group text every Wednesday evening.

1. Read /home/workspace/Contacts/family-context.md, which lists low-stakes safe topics, shared interests, and topics to avoid.
2. Pick one safe topic at random, weighted toward anything not used in the last 30 days (check the file's 'last_used' notes if present).
3. Optionally run a single web_search for one current, friendly, non-political item on that topic. If nothing clean turns up, skip the news angle.
4. Draft one warm opener, one or two sentences, casual, no questions stacked on top of each other.
5. SMS me via send_sms_to_user with just the draft text. No 'Here is your opener', no labels, no quotes.

Never touch anything in the 'avoid' list, even adjacent.
expectedOutput: A single Wednesday evening SMS containing one ready-to-paste opener, nothing else.
customization: Edit family-context.md to control safe topics, the avoid list, or the rotation window.

name: Dog Walk Route Randomizer
prompt:
Pick the right dog walk for today's weather and the rotation, and text it to me.

1. Read /home/workspace/Pets/walk-routes.md (route name, length, terrain, shade, and last_done date).
2. Run one web_search for today's forecast high and precipitation in Salmon, Idaho.
3. Apply rules in order:
   - High above 80F: pick a shaded route under 2 miles.
   - High below 30F or precipitation likely: pick a short low-elevation route.
   - Otherwise: pick any route the dog has not done in the last 5 days.
4. SMS me via send_sms_to_user with three lines: route name, expected length, and a one-line reason (for example 'shaded, 86F forecast').

If nothing qualifies under the rotation rule, repeat the most recent shaded route and say so.
expectedOutput: A morning SMS naming one walk route with its length and a one-line reason it was picked.
customization: Edit walk-routes.md or tune the temperature thresholds and rotation window.

name: Monthly Phone Photo Backup Nudge
prompt:
Check whether my phone photos are still being backed up, once a month.

1. Use use_app_google_drive to list files in the 'Phone Backups' folder, sorted by created/modified date.
2. Find the most recent upload date across the folder.
3. If the newest file is older than 35 days, email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Phone photos not backed up in over a month'. Body includes the gap in days and a three-item checklist: plug in phone, open backup app, verify folder size grew.
4. If the newest file is within 35 days, email me with subject 'Photo backups look current' and a single line stating the date of the most recent upload.

If the folder is missing or empty, treat it as a stale backup and send the warning email.
expectedOutput: A monthly email that either nudges with a three-step checklist or confirms the last backup date.
customization: Change the Drive folder name, the 35-day threshold, or the checklist steps.

name: The Lazy What I Ate Log
prompt:
Twice a day, log what I ate. No analysis, no scoring, no lectures.

1. SMS me via send_sms_to_user with exactly 'What did you eat in the last few hours? One line, lazy is fine.'
2. Wait for my reply.
3. Append a row to /home/workspace/Health/food-log.csv with columns timestamp, slot, text. The 1pm run writes slot=midday, the 8pm run writes slot=evening. If the file does not exist, create it with that header.
4. If I do not reply within the agent window, write a row with text='(no reply)' so the gap is visible in the log.

Do not summarize the entry back to me. Do not comment on what I ate. Do not send a confirmation text.
expectedOutput: Two short SMS prompts a day and a growing CSV log of what I actually ate, with no commentary.
customization: Change the wording, the number of pings per day, or the slot labels in the CSV.

name: Plant Watering Days Counter
prompt:
Text me only on days a plant is actually due.

1. Read /home/workspace/Home/plants.md (each plant has name, location, and watering interval in days).
2. Read /home/workspace/Home/plant-log.csv and find the latest last_watered date per plant.
3. For each plant where today is greater than last_watered plus interval, build a line like 'snake plant (camper window) is due'.
4. If at least one line exists, send one combined SMS via send_sms_to_user with one plant per line. If nothing is due, send nothing.
5. Do not write back to plant-log.csv. I update that manually when I actually water.

If a plant has no entry in plant-log.csv yet, treat it as due and include it on the first run.
expectedOutput: A conditional morning SMS listing only the plants actually due, one per line.
customization: Edit plants.md to add plants and intervals, or split alerts into indoor and outdoor.

name: Friday Wind-Down Playlist
prompt:
Build a 12-track Friday wind-down playlist that matches my actual taste.

1. Read /home/workspace/Personal/music-taste.md (liked genres, anchor artists, and a 'do not include' list).
2. Decide on a 'slow Friday evening' mood: lower tempo, fewer vocals up front, nothing punishing.
3. Pick 12 tracks across at least 6 different artists. Pull from the anchor artists and adjacent acts in the same genres.
4. If unsure whether a track or album exists, run a web_search to confirm. Do not invent tracks. If you cannot confirm, replace it with a well-known catalog song from the same artist.
5. Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Friday wind-down, 12 tracks' and a numbered markdown list in the form 'Artist - Title'. No commentary, no genre tags.

Never include anything on the 'do not include' list.
expectedOutput: A Friday evening email with a numbered 12-track playlist in 'Artist - Title' format and nothing else.
customization: Edit music-taste.md, change the track count, or shift the mood (focus, drive, deep work).

name: Tax Receipt Photo Catcher
prompt:
Catch new tax receipts in Drive and add them to my ledger every Sunday.

1. Read /home/workspace/Finance/receipts-state.json to get last_run timestamp. If the file is missing, treat last_run as 30 days ago.
2. Use use_app_google_drive to list files in the 'Receipts inbox' folder created since last_run.
3. For each new file, append a row to /home/workspace/Finance/receipts.csv with columns date_added, filename, drive_link, category (left blank). Create the file with that header if it does not exist.
4. Update last_run in receipts-state.json to now.
5. Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Receipts filed - [today]'. Body is one line with the count, then the first five filenames as a bullet list for spot checking.

Do not guess categories. I tag those by hand.
expectedOutput: A Sunday email confirming the count of new receipts filed plus a sample of filenames, with the CSV and state file updated.
customization: Change the Drive folder name, the email cadence, or add automatic category guessing if you trust it.

name: Car Maintenance Mileage Nudge
prompt:
Run a Monday maintenance check on the truck.

1. Read /home/workspace/Home/vehicle.md. Required fields: current odometer (manually updated), last_oil_change_miles, oil_interval, last_tire_rotation_miles, rotation_interval, registration_due (YYYY-MM-DD).
2. Compute miles since last oil change. Flag 'Oil change' if (odometer - last_oil_change_miles) is greater than (oil_interval - 300).
3. Compute miles since last tire rotation. Flag 'Tire rotation' the same way using rotation_interval.
4. Compute days until registration_due. Flag 'Registration' if 30 days or fewer.
5. If any flags exist, email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Camper truck maintenance due soon' and a bullet list naming each flagged item with the specific number that triggered it.
6. If nothing is flagged, SMS me via send_sms_to_user with exactly 'Truck is current.'

Do not send both an email and an SMS in the same run.
expectedOutput: A weekly conditional email when service is approaching, or a single short SMS when everything is current.
customization: Update vehicle.md with new intervals, add brake pad mileage, or change the lead-time buffers.

name: Friend Birthday Card Drafter
prompt:
Read /home/workspace/Contacts/friends.md, which has columns for name, birthday, vibe (dry, sincere, goofy), and a short notes field.

1. Find every person whose birthday is exactly 3 days from today. If none match, stop and produce no output.
2. For each match, draft a single handwritten-card message between 3 and 5 sentences. Match the vibe column exactly. Pull at least one specific detail from the notes column into the message so it cannot have been written for anyone else.
3. Keep each draft as a separate block under an H2 with the person's name and the actual birthday date.

Email me all drafts together via send_email_to_user with subject 'Card drafts to write today'. No preamble, no summary line at the top, just the drafts.
expectedOutput: An email three days before a friend's birthday with one card draft per matching person, written in their vibe and grounded in a real detail from your notes.
customization: Adjust the lead time, the set of vibe categories, or the sentence count range.

name: Hourly Move Your Body Ping
prompt:
Read /home/workspace/Health/micro-movements.md, which lists 20 movements (each 30 to 90 seconds, no equipment, low impact).

1. Read /home/workspace/Health/micro-movements-log.csv and find every movement used in the last 4 hours.
2. Pick one movement from the markdown file that is not in that recent set. If every movement was used recently, pick the one used longest ago.
3. Append a row to the CSV with timestamp, movement name, and duration.
4. Text me via send_sms_to_user with only the movement name and the duration, nothing else. No greeting, no encouragement, no emoji.

Tone is flat and matter of fact. If the file is missing or empty, do nothing.
expectedOutput: A short workday SMS each hour with one micro-movement and its duration, rotating without repeats inside a 4 hour window.
customization: Edit micro-movements.md, change the active hours, or pause the agent on focus-block days.

name: Saturday Farmers Market Forecast
prompt:
Build a Saturday morning market briefing.

1. Run web_search for the current week's farmers market hours and location in Salmon, Idaho. If you cannot confirm hours from a primary source (city site, market site, local paper), say so explicitly instead of guessing.
2. Run web_search for the Saturday morning weather forecast for Salmon, Idaho. Capture temperature range, precipitation chance, and wind.
3. Read /home/workspace/Home/market-staples.md. Filter to items tagged with the current season.
4. Compose a short email: market hours and location at top, weather block in the middle, shopping list at the bottom as a bullet list.

Email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Saturday market notes'. Keep it under 200 words total.
expectedOutput: A short Saturday morning email with confirmed market hours, the morning forecast, and a seasonal shopping list pulled from your staples file.
customization: Edit market-staples.md per season, change the city, or drop the weather section if unwanted.

name: Holiday Gift List Builder
prompt:
Build the holiday gift plan for the year.

1. Read /home/workspace/Contacts/friends.md and /home/workspace/Contacts/family-context.md. Expect a 'tier' column with values under-25, 25-75, or 75-plus.
2. For each person, write a one line vibe summary based on their notes, then propose exactly 3 gift ideas. Every idea must reference something concrete from the notes (a hobby, a recent project, a stated want). No generic mugs, candles, or socks.
3. Group the sheet by tier, then alphabetical by name inside each tier. Include a running total of estimated spend per tier at the bottom.

Write the full sheet to /home/workspace/Personal/gift-list-<year>.md using create_or_rewrite_file. Then email me via send_email_to_user with subject 'Holiday gift list ready' containing the file path, the count of people covered, and the per-tier spend totals.
expectedOutput: A November 1 email pointing at a fully drafted gift list on disk, grouped by spend tier, with three grounded ideas per person.
customization: Change the tier thresholds, expand to coworkers or neighbors, or move the kickoff date to October.

name: Vercel Deploy Failure Sentinel
prompt:
Watch Vercel for new deploy failures.

1. In bash, curl https://api.vercel.com/v6/deployments?limit=20 with header 'Authorization: Bearer $VERCEL_TOKEN'. Parse JSON.
2. Read /home/.z/state/vercel-deploys.json to get the last_seen_created timestamp. If the file is missing, create it with the newest deployment's createdAt and exit without alerting (first run is a baseline, not a flood).
3. Filter to deployments where state is ERROR, CANCELED, or BUILD_ERROR and createdAt is newer than last_seen_created.
4. For each new failure, text me via send_sms_to_user with one line: '<project> <branch> failed at <time UTC>, <inspectorUrl>'. One SMS per failure, no batching.
5. Update /home/.z/state/vercel-deploys.json with the newest createdAt seen this run.

Do not alert on READY, QUEUED, or BUILDING.
expectedOutput: A near real-time SMS only when a Vercel build fails, including the project, branch, time, and inspector link. Silent otherwise.
customization: Restrict to specific project IDs, change the poll interval, or also POST to a Slack webhook.

name: Supabase Row Count Drift Watch
prompt:
